coredata.dc:title: Ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence carbon dots-based composites with high environmental stability
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Bingfeng
Sun, Wansong
Liu, Qian
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials occupy an extremely important position in many applications, and how to achieve stable phosphorescent emission to the environment is still a huge challenge. In this article, citric acid (CA) derived carbon dots (CACDs) are firmly embedded in the melamine (MA)-derived crystals by covalently cross-linking way through a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained CA-MA-3 composite has an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 760 ms with an absolute quantum yield of 11.24%. The RTP should originate from the possible formation of hydrogen and covalent bonds between CACDs and MA-derived crystals. More importantly, the composite exhibits super-stable RTP emission in organic solvents and aqueous solutions with pH = 1 or 14. Because of the excellent phosphorescence capability of the CA-MA-3, it can be widely used for anti-counterfeiting security under extremely harsh conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescent
Carbon dots
Hydrogen bonds
Aqueous stability
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22271044 ) and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Foundation (No. 20210101404JC ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NMR spectra of MA-derived crystals (a) 1H NMR, (b) 13C NMR, and (c) the chemical structure of the substance produced during the reaction.
(a) TEM images of CA-MA-3. (b) TEM images of CA-MA-3 etched in DMSO and (c) HRTEM of (b). (d) FTIR spectra of different samples.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s for CA-MA-3.

(a) Phosphorescence excitation spectrum of CA-MA-3 powder with emission at 489 nm and UV–vis absorption spectra of CA-MA-3 and MA raw. Phosphorescence spectra for (b) CA-MA-3 and (c) MA-A in solid excited by various wavelengths of light.

(a) Images of CA-MA-3 under daylight, UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF with time. (b) RTP decay curve of the CA-MA-3 powder, recorded at 486 nm emission under excitation at 270 nm. (c) TGA curves of MA-A and CA-MA-3.
(a) Safety protection application of CA-MA-3 composite solid. (b) Anticounterfeiting application of CA-MA-3 water-based varnish.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CA-MA composite with FL and RTP emission properties in the solid and its internal structure, and the possible energy structure and RTP emission process.

head.ce:title: Ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence carbon dots-based composites with high environmental stability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials occupy an extremely important position in many applications, and how to achieve stable phosphorescent emission to the environment is still a huge challenge. In this article, citric acid (CA) derived carbon dots (CACDs) are firmly embedded in the melamine (MA)-derived crystals by covalently cross-linking way through a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained CA-MA-3 composite has an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 760 ms with an absolute quantum yield of 11.24%. The RTP should originate from the possible formation of hydrogen and covalent bonds between CACDs and MA-derived crystals. More importantly, the composite exhibits super-stable RTP emission in organic solvents and aqueous solutions with pH = 1 or 14. Because of the excellent phosphorescence capability of the CA-MA-3, it can be widely used for anti-counterfeiting security under extremely harsh conditions.

CA-derived CDs were in situ embedded into MA-derived crystals through one-step hydrothermal method. The resultant CA-MA-3 composite shows ultra-long RTP lifetime and 11.24% QYs due to the generation of covalent crosslinking and hydrogen bonds between CACDs and crystals. CA-MA-3 composite exhibits super-stable RTP emission in aqueous solutions with pH = 1 or 14 with potential applications in anti-counterfeiting security.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has received increasing attention for its high signal-to-noise ratio, significant Stokes shift, and remarkable luminescence lifetime in recent years [1–3]. RTP materials can be widely used in various fields such as biological imaging [4–6], sensing [7,8], and information security [9,10]. Metal complexes are the earliest RTP materials discovered and used, but, the high toxicity and cost, and scarce resources have limited further research on RTP materials [1,2,11–14]. The emergence of sustainable, easily modifiable organic metal-free RTP materials offers a solution to these problems [1–3]. Metal-free organic RTP materials are generally including small molecules and polymers, which have the properties of easy processing, low cost, and long lifetime [15,16]. There are two common means to realize RTP: one is to advance the intersystem crossing of singlet-triplet excited states by enhancing the spin-orbit coupling, and the other is to suppress the non-radiative transition of the triplet state, thus reducing the deactivation of the triplet excitons [12,17,18]. However, the excited triplet state is easily quenched in an aerobic and aqueous environment, and RTP emission of these materials can only be discovered in an anhydrous solid state, which largely limits the application of current organic RTP materials [5,11,13,19]. Therefore, it is challenging to construct environmentally friendly and stable RTP materials in harsh environments such as water through a facile and effective method.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new breed of luminescent nanomaterial, are widely used in bioimaging [6,11,20,21], light-emitting devices [22,23], photocatalysis [24,25] and other fields due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and low-cost [20,21,26–28]. Especially, CDs exhibit excellent photo-tunability to achieve fluorescence emission from blue to red, and most CDs have excitation-dependent photoluminescence properties. However, the instability of the triplet excited state and the difficulty of intersystem crossing limited the phosphorescence in such luminescence material [29,30]. Therefore, obtaining RTP CDs remain an immense challenge. The methods to achieve effective RTP for CDs can be broadly classified into the following categories: (1) Building hydrogen bond networks. In 2013, Deng et al. introduced CDs into polyvinyl alcohol for the first time to carry out RTP by constructing hydrogen bonds, which can be successfully applied to information encryption [19]. And then, Li and coworkers constructed a hydrogen bonds network between CDs and cyanuric acid (CCA) using water molecules, which can be successfully used for the detection of RTP in an aqueous environment [31]. Recently, Gao's group reported the hydrogen bonds network through CDs and melamine (MA) to limit the triplet exciton non-radiative decay of CDs, promoting the emission of RTP in the aqueous environment [32]. (2) The RTP emission was achieved by doping CDs into different solid matrices such as polyurethane [33], composite matrix (urea and biuret) [17], molecular sieve [34], KAl(SO4)2·xH2O [35], silica gel [36], and boric acid [37] to control the motion of groups on the surface of CDs and suppress the non-radiative transitions. Nevertheless, these embedded matrices are less restrictive for CDs, resulting in a relatively low RTP lifetime. Nowadays, the matrices have been extended to zeolites [38], two-dimensional layered double hydroxide (LDH) [39,40], metal-organic framework (MOF) [41], and NaCl hybrid crystals [42]. These more rigid matrices enable CDs to generate stable RTP emission by hindering intermolecular vibration and isolating the CDs from oxygen. (3) Immobilizing CDs using covalent bonds for stable RTP emission. In 2019, Li and his partners incorporated CDs into SiO2 using covalent bonds and observed ultra-long RTP in both liquid and solid [43]. Recently, Sun et al. prepared RTP material with a lifetime of 5.72 s. The multiple restrictions by highly rigid networks, steady covalent bonds, and 3D space confinement efficaciously limited the deactivation of non-radiative triplet excited states caused by CDs. However, the preparation and synthesis processes are more cumbersome [44].

Benefiting from the abundant amino groups and stable chemical properties, MA has been utilized as an excellent candidate for hydrogen bond interactions with molecules having carboxyl or hydroxyl groups in recent years. In 2018, Bian and coworkers established a rigid environment based on MA and aromatic acid that locks molecules firmly in the three-dimensional framework through their self-assembly behavior in an aqueous medium, which effectively limits the non-radiative decay of the triplet state and facilitates intersystem crossing [45]. In addition, Zhou et al. proposed RTP by self-assembling crystals through hydrogen bonds between MA and multiple dibasic acids [13]. At present, the materials based on MA and carbon dots have been widely reported based on the simple low-temperature hydrothermal treatment, but, stable RTP materials in extreme environments are still scarce.

Herein, an in situ strategy for the construction of citric acid (CA)-derived CDs embedded in melamine (MA)-derived crystal composites (CACDs-MA) with extremely strong and stable RTP emission in organic and aqueous environments. The CACDs-MA shows an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 760 ms under 270 nm excitation and the RTP of solid CACDs-MA cannot be quenched in an aqueous solution with pH = 1 or 14, which is a feature rarely reported before. Further studies have shown that CACDs were embedded in the MA crystal by covalent crosslinking, which hinders vibration and rotation facilitating stable emission of phosphorescence. The formation of hydrogen bonds in the composite material provides the possibility for the generation of RTP. Based on the super-stable RTP characteristic, the as-fabricated composite can provide an expansive field for the application of phosphorescence, such as solid anti-counterfeiting security.

Citric acid (CA), melamine (MA), and cyanuric acid (CCA) were obtained from Aladdin. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and other reagents used in this work were purchased from Macklin. All purchased chemical reagents were analytically pure and purified for use from commercial suppliers.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted with the Japan JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were achieved on a Quantum 2000 spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on SHIMADZU UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was implemented with Magna 560 FTIR spectrometer. Phosphorescence and fluorescence tests were carried out on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer at ambient conditions. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was observed by TA Q50 thermal analyzer (condition: N2 atmosphere, heating rate of 10 °C/min). Time-resolved luminescence spectra were recorded on the Hitachi F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare CACDs-MA composites from CA-MA-1 to CA-MA-5 using various molar ratios (10:1, 5:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2) of MA/CA. Typically, the sample CA-MA-3 was synthesized as follows: Melamine (0.48 g, 3.80 mmol) and citric acid (0.366 g, 1.90 mmol) were put into 15 mL deionized water and repeatedly stirred to mix well. Subsequently, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-line reactor before being heated at 200 °C for 6 h, then automatically cooling down to room temperature. Afterward, the reaction product was centrifuged to remove impurities with deionized water several times, and vacuum dried to obtain the solid powder CA-MA-3. The synthetic procedure of other CA-MA samples was the same as above.

As a control sample, the CA-derived CDs were also directed embedded into MA matrix by hydrothermal route. Firstly, CA-derived CDs were synthesized by a common pyrolysis reaction. CA (4.0 g) was placed in a three-necked flask and used an electric heating mantle to heat it to 220 °C for 25 min. After the sample has cooled, appending 10 mL of deionized water was to the sample to dissolve the solids, and then pH was adjusted to neutral with 1.0 M NaOH solution. Subsequently, the unreacted small molecules were removed by dialyzing from the above solution with the dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 36 h before lyophilized to acquire orange solid CDs. Secondly, CACDs were encapsulated into MA crystals through a hydrothermal reaction. In brief, MA (0.85 g) and CACDs (2 mg) in 15 mL deionized water were added into a Teflon-line reaction kettle before the solution was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting product (CACDs@MA) was washed with deionized water several times and centrifuged before eventually the product was got by vacuum drying.

Firstly, the pure MA-derived crystal MA-A was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 6 h. The 1H NMR spectrum of MA-A measured in DMSO‑d 6 is shown in Fig. 1 a. It can be observed that the characteristic peak of –NH2 of MA appears at 5.96 ppm, and the characteristic signal peak at 11.16 ppm can be ascribed to the –OH of cyanuric acid (CCA). The signal peaks at c, d, and e appear at 6.65, 10.46, and 9.95 ppm, respectively, which are attributed to –NH, –NH2 of cyanuric acid amide and –NH, –NH2 of cyanuric acid amide. According to the integral area of a and b, the MA: CCA = 1:1 was calculated with a low molar content (<5.5%) of cyanuric acid amide and cyanuric acid amide. Fig. 1b shows the 13C NMR spectrum of MA-derived crystal MA-A. The C signal peak of MA appears at 167.91 ppm, and the characteristic C peak of CCA is at 150.43 ppm. The above structural characterizations demonstrate that the MA-A sample is mainly composed of a co-crystal formed by MA and CCA [46–48]. The CACDs-MA composites were obtained with various molar ratios (10:1, 5:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2) of MA and CA by a one-step hydrothermal route at 200 °C for 6 h, which were recorded as CA-MA-1 to CA-MA-5 respectively. All samples prepared were in crystalline solid form in appearance. In addition, the intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions were further certified by 1H NMR spectra. As shown in Fig. S1a, the proton peaks of CA-MA-3 are significantly shifted toward a low electric field, compared with the respective proton peaks of CCA and MA in the DMSO‑d 6 solvent. These differences in proton shift changes may reveal the existence of intermolecular hydrogen bonds in CA-MA-3. We studied the solid-state 13C NMR of CA-MA-3 as shown in Fig. S1b. The signal peaks at 166.1 ppm and 154.7 ppm are ascribed to the MA and CCA units in the composite. The peak at 160.6 ppm may be originated from the sp2 hybridized carbons bonded to heteroatoms in the cross-linked network structure formed by MA and CDs. Furthermore, the other carbon signals of small residual molecules are almost not observed.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the TEM image was conducted to observe the morphologies of the representative sample CA-MA-3 prepared with a 2:1 M ratio of MA/CA, which has a typical solid form of blocky morphology without small nanodots. This may be owing to the formation of a hydrogen bond network in CA-MA-3 after hydrothermal treatment. It is universally acknowledged that MA crystals are slightly soluble in water, but the ammonium salts are poorly soluble in water at room temperature which was prepared through mix both MA and CA into water. In addition, MA is difficult to decompose even at 200 °C as a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic molecule. To demonstrate this, a control sample of MA-A based on MA with the same synthesis conditions as CA-MA-3 was prepared. As shown in Fig. S2a, the TEM morphology of MA-A is similar to CA-MA-3. Different from the MA molecule, previous studies have shown that CA molecules form small carbon nanodots at a temperature above 160–170 °C [49,50]. Therefore, we speculated that CA-MA-3 is formed by CA-derived carbon dots first and then wrapped by MA crystals. To demonstrate the hypothesis, we etched the MA crystals at 90 °C with DMSO as the solvent and dropped the obtained solution on a copper grid to observe the TEM morphology. In Fig. 2b, the TEM image testifies the size of the plentiful small CA-derived carbon dots is 2–5 nm, with lattice fringes of about 0.18 nm (Fig. 2c). The CDs obtained from etched CA-MA-3 composite have a larger size (2–5 nm) than the CDs synthesized by the pyrolysis of pure CA with a uniform size of 2.09 ± 0.04 nm (Fig. S2b). Nevertheless, the highly consistent lattice spacing of the two CDs shows that they are mainly derived from the CA molecules (Fig. 2c and Fig. S2c). The CA-derived CDs were cross-linked and encapsulated by MA, resulting in a larger size of the embedded CDs in the CA-MA-3 composites. Significantly, the successful and uniform incorporation of the in situ-formed CA-derived CDs into the MA crystals was confirmed by the above results. Meanwhile, the microstructure of CA-MA-3 without etching and after etching with DMSO was displayed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. S3a, we can observe the crystal morphology of unetched CA-MA-3. While Fig. S3b shows many uniform small particles after etching with DMSO, verifying the formation of cross-linked structures in CA-MA-3. Simultaneously, to demonstrate the effect of molecular luminophores in CA-MA-3, we etched crystalline CA-MA-3 at 90 °C in DMSO solvent. The mass of CA-MA-3 was measured before and after etching, and the mass change of composite material is very small before and after etching. In addition, the 1H NMR characterization (Fig. S4) for the residual solids and mother liquor after etching also indicated that molecular state luminophores or small molecules accounted for less than 3% estimated by integral area, which is consistent with the solid-state 13C NMR results of CA-MA-3 (Fig. S1b). So, it can be seen that there are very few molecular states or intermediate molecules in composites. These molecular state intermediates adsorbed on the composite particles can be washed by the deionized water as described in the experimental section. While within the crosslinked composite the CDs as crosslinker was formed by almost fully carbonized CA molecules at a high temperature of 200 °C.

Furthermore, we employed the XRD characterization to clarify the crystal structures of CA-MA-3 and MA crystals obtained from different hydrothermal conditions. As shown in Fig. S5, the crystal structure of MA with hydrothermally treated at 150 °C and 200 °C displayed differences in some XRD patterns. Especially, the appearance of a new diffraction peak at 10.9° and the disappearance of some main peaks at 13.1° (101) and 17.5° (011) indicated the crystal structure of MA-A was almost similar to the CA-MA-3 [51,52]. To explore the chemical composition of different materials, FTIR was further used to analyze the chemical composition of different materials. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the characteristic peaks of raw MA can be observed at 3467 and 3415 cm−1 (-NH2 antisymmetric stretching vibration), 1664 cm−1 (NH bending vibration), 1528 cm−1 (CN stretching vibration of triazine ring), 809 cm−1 (deformation vibration of triazine ring) [53–55]. The disappearance of the peak at 3464 cm−1 of the amino group and the appearance of a broad peak between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 suggested the existence of intermolecular hydrogen bonds in CA-MA-3. For MA-A, the stretching vibrations of –NH2 groups disappeared and the characteristic peaks associated with –NH2 or –OH groups can be seen, demonstrating the formation of hydrogen bond interaction. In addition, a new FTIR peak at 1728 cm−1 associated with the carbonyl groups of amide linkage (-CO-NH) can be found, indicating that the MA may be hydrolyzed into amino-1,3,5-triazine-2,4(1H, 3H)-dione (ammelide) or cyanuric acid diamide (ammeline) after hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 6 h. Partial hydrolysis of MA molecules has been found in MA-D(150 °C for 6 h) and MA-B(200 °C for 3 h), although some characteristic peaks of MA are still preserved. In addition, the XRD patterns (Fig. S5) of these samples also indirectly supported the conclusion. MA, as the embedded matrix of CDs, will be hydrolyzed and a small amount of it may be reacted with CA to fabricate CDs under the high-temperature hydrothermal conditions, which caused the FTIR curves of CA-MA-3 and CACDs@MA composites similar to that of MA-A.

The chemical structure and compositions of CA-MA-3 and MA-A were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 3 and Fig. S6). The results of XPS experiments indicate that the CA-MA-3 mainly consists of C 1s (283 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (531 eV) (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3b) of C 1s can be divided into three peaks at 284.4, 285.6 and 288.5 eV for C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CN/CO, respectively [56]. The CN/CO bonds were also discovered in MA-A (Fig. S6b), and the CO bonds appeared in the high-resolution O 1s spectra of both CA-MA-3 and MA-A at 531.7 eV (Fig. 3d and Fig. S6d), suggesting that the MA matrix has been hydrolyzed into ammelide or ammeline [27,57], which is agreeing with that of FTIR and XRD. Additionally, the XPS data showed the atom ratio of N/O is close to 2:1, indicating that the MA crystals should be hydrolyzed into ammelide in the hydrothermal reaction. As shown in Fig. 2c, the N 1s spectrum of CA-MA-3 uncovers the presence of N atoms mainly in the form of pyridinic N (CN) at 398.6 eV, C–NH–C at 399.7 eV, C–NH2 at 400.6 eV, and graphitic N at 401.8 eV, respectively. In contrast to MA-A, a new peak ascribed to the graphitic N of CACDs appears in CA-MA-3 (Fig. S6c). Thus, the result shows that the formation of CDs from CA molecules by carbonization was successfully embedded in organic crystals. Above all, these above characterizations evidenced the CA-derived CDs were well embedded in the MA matrix after the high-temperature hydrolyzed of co-crystallization (mainly cyanuric acid and a small amount of cyanuric acid monoamide and cyanuric acid diamide).

The photophysical properties of the CACDs-MA composites were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of MA raw indicated a typical absorption peak at 277 nm due to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. The n→π* transition originating from CO and CN bonds also makes MA raw exhibit an absorption band at 320–550 nm [31,58–60]. The main absorption of CA-MA-3 is at 273 nm, which also shows enhanced light absorption at 320–550 nm compared to MA raw. Afterward, the as-prepared CA-MA, MA-A, and CACDs@MA samples reveal satisfactory fluorescence characteristics (Fig. S7). These samples at reaction conditions of 200 °C for 6 h exhibited no excitation-dependent emission properties in FL emission spectra, accompanied by a bright blue fluorescence under 430–480 nm. Especially, the MA-A presented a large red-shifted PL emission peak at 450 nm, indicating that MA crystals are essentially changed under high-temperature hydrothermal conditions as demonstrated in the above analysis (Fig. S7a) compared to a deep blue emission of the MA raw at 367 nm (Fig. S8a). Furthermore, for the CA-MA series samples with different MA/CA ratios (Figs. S7b–d), the CA-MA-4 composite with MA/CA = 1:1 displayed maximum fluorescence emission intensity due to the equivalent ratio of the amino groups for MA and carboxyl functional groups for CA. The in situ formed CACDs could be covalently embedded into MA-derived crystals, but the fluorescence intensity of CA-MA-5 is significantly decreased on account of the excessive content of CACDs leading to the aggregation of CDs in organic crystals. The PL intensity of CA-MA-3 or CA-MA-4 was improved compared to CACDs and CACDs@MA, which means that the in-situ generation method of CACDs in crystals is beneficial to the optical properties of the composites owing to the better covalent hybridization.

To obtain insight into the RTP properties of CA-MA-3, a series of photophysical properties were characterized (Tables S1–S2). The phosphorescence decay curve was conducted and fitted by an exponential function with three lifetime components: 2 ms (88%), 220 ms (2.9%) and 820 ms (9.3%) (Fig. 5b), and the average phosphorescence lifetime was calculated to be 760 ms (excitation at 270 nm) on the basis of the formula: τavg = ΣAiτi 2/Aiτi [37,61]. And CA-MA-3 exhibits a relatively high absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (QYP) of 11.24% (Table S1). The fluorescence decay curve was conducted and fitted by an exponential function with double lifetime components: 4.83 ns (64%) and 9.24 ns (36%) (Fig. 5b), and the average fluorescence lifetime (τF) was calculated to be 6.4 ns (excitation at 270 nm) (Fig. S13). In Table S2, we can find that the radiative decay rate kr,F of the fluorescence of CA-MA-3 is much greater than its non-radiative decay rate knr,F, which means that its fluorescence emission is mainly based on radiative transitions. The non-radiative decay rate knr,P of phosphorescence is about 10 times higher than its radiative decay rate kr,P, which means that the RTP performance of CA-MA-3 mainly comes from its non-radiative decay [14,61]. Compared with other reported results (Table S3), the CA-MA-3 developed in this study is based on a simpler method prepared by hydrothermal route with comparable RTP performance. Significantly, the RTP intensity of MA-A at 498 nm also shows an ultra-long lifetime of 0.79 s (Fig. S14a), which supported that the MA crystals changed to other substances (ammelide). To further prove this, the effect of different hydrothermal treatment temperatures and time on the optical properties of different MA samples was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S12b, CA-MA composites showed an enhanced RTP intensity with increasing hydrothermal treatment temperature of pure MA from 20 min (MA-C) to 6 h (MA-A) at 200 °C. Interestingly, when MA raw was heated at 200 °C for 6 h without water as a solvent, the obtained MA-E has very weak RTP. From the above results, it can be inferred that the heat treatment of MA in the absence of water will show poor phosphorescence performance. Therefore, as demonstrated by NMR, IR, XRD, and XPS above, the structure of MA was gradually transformed to cyanuric acid amide under hydrothermal treatment, providing hydrogen bonds and amide bonds for the reaction to promote phosphorescence emission [63].

In addition, as a comparison, we also studied the RTP property of the CACDs@MA composite obtained by the direct hydrothermal synthesis of CACDs and MA at 200 °C for 6 h. As shown in Figs. S12c and d, CACDs hardly exhibited room-temperature phosphorescence. but were embedded in an MA-derived matrix to achieve notable improvement in RTP performance for the CACDs@MA with a broad blue phosphorescence emission centered at 460 nm and an ultra-long lifetime of 0.86 s (Fig. S14b). However, compared with CA-MA-3, the CACDs@MA has lower RTP properties, especially without an extra phosphorescence emission peak at 400 nm. So, the synthesis method of composites by embedding CACDs into MA-derived crystals has an important influence on the phosphorescence performance of the resulting composite. The in-situ formation method can make the CDs evenly dispersed in the organic crystals with better covalent hybridization between CDs and matrix without aggregation, and obtain the dual-channel high-efficiency blue RTP composite.

To study the stability of the CA-MA-3 composite obtained by in situ embedding CACDs in MA-derived crystals, the changes in the phosphorescence of CA-MA-3 in water, acid and alkali solution, and organic solvents were explored (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16). It is observed that when dispersing CA-MA-3 (2 mg) in 5 mL water or aqueous solution with pH = 1 or 14 by ultrasound, the resulting aqueous dispersion of CA-MA-3 remained stable RTP after seven days at room temperature. Figs. S15b and c present the PL and RTP spectra of CA-MA-3 dispersed in water. As shown in Fig. S16, the RTP intensities of CA-MA-3 under 270 nm excitation remained almost unchanged after 7 days of storage in water, acid and alkali solutions, and organic solvents (represented by acetone), indicating that CA-MA-3 exhibits excellent environmental stability. Above spectral measurement and SEM patterns (Fig. S15 d, e) can show that the sample is not damaged and the original RTP performance is maintained.

The excellent stability of CA-MA-3 suggests that the in situ formed CA-derived CDs with carboxyl groups may produce a cross-linked structure with melamine at the initial stage of the reaction through forming an amidation reaction and hydrogen bonding during the heating process (Scheme 1). The cross-linked structure shortens the distance between functional groups and enhances the electron overlap in the nano-confined space, inhibiting the non-radiative transition of the triplet excitons, promoting the crossover between systems, enhancing the fluorescence emission and preventing the quenching of phosphorescence [64,65]. With the progress of the high-temperature reaction, the MA hydrolysis reaction occurred in water. FTIR and XPS data demonstrated that MA was mostly hydrolyzed to cyanuric acid, which is more likely to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Therefore, we speculate that the CA-derived carbon nucleus is firmly wrapped in the outer layer by the hydrogen bonds network after being immobilized by MA, which promotes the transition of T1 and the emission of phosphorescence has been enhanced. The multi-layer protection provides the possibility of stable RTP emission of CA-MA-3 in various environments including strong acid or strong base. The stability of the CA-MA-3 material in different solvents (water, methanol, ethanol, acetone, formaldehyde, dichloromethane, and tetrahydrofuran) was investigated. The phosphorescence was also observed despite the insolubility of the solid in the solvents (Fig. S17). This indicates that CACDs and MA-derived matrix are tightly bound through covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds, and the organic crystals play a critical role in protection and immobilization of CDs, inhibiting the vibration and rotation between molecules. Therefore, even in strongly polar DMSO solvent, the structure of the crystallized CA-MA-3 could not be destroyed in organic solvents. Interestingly, MA raw, CA-MA-3, CA-MA-4, MA-A, and MA-B were added into DMSO at 90 °C for 30 min, the experiments showed that the phosphorescence was quenched in all samples except CA-MA-3 and CA-MA-4 (Fig. S18). This should be due to the following reasons: (1) the structure of MA-derived crystals for all samples are destroyed by DMSO solvent, resulting in phosphorescence quenching; (2) the cross-linking occurs between CACDs and crystals during the reaction; (3) the cyanuric acid produced by high-temperature hydrolysis can further form a stable hydrogen bonds network with MA or themselves to promote phosphorescence emission.

The thermal stability of the CA-MA-3 composite was further studied by using the TGA. MA-A and CA-MA-3 have almost the same crystal structure and synthesis steps except for the in situ high-temperature carbonization of CA to form CDs, so we chose these two samples for temperature stability test. As shown in Fig. 5c, the MA-A and CA-MA-3 showed no mass loss and the main degradation step starts at 300 °C, owing to no residual water molecules. The final weight loss of MA is almost 100%, while there are still about 3 wt% residues for the CA-MA-3 composite, which further confirms the conclusion we got in the NMR test at Fig. S1b and Fig. S4. The above result also indicates that the formation of hydrogen bonds in the CA-MA-3 composite is done without the presence of water. Our work is different from the previously reported RTP systems with strong water-assisted hydrogen bond networks [31,32,45]. Due to the involvement of water molecules in the hydrogen bonds, the RTP intensity of the as-synthesized materials was affected by the water content [31,32]. The comparison manifests that CA-MA-3 has outstanding thermal stability owing to the absence of water molecules involved in the construction of hydrogen bonds. At the same time, it further proves that the amidation reaction produces a cross-linked structure, thereby promoting the RTP emission of the composite.

So far, most of the reported RTP materials only can achieve phosphorescence under dry conditions rather than in an aqueous solution. In this work, the designed and synthesized CA-MA-3 exhibits superior solvent stability. The lotus pattern in Fig. 6 a is depicted by the solid powder of CA-MA-3, which exhibits stable solid phosphorescence properties after excitation by 365 nm. The CA-MA-3 was mixed with water-based varnish (WV) to obtain CA-MA-3-WV. According to Fig. 6b, the digital pattern “8888” was written by WV and CA-MA-3-WV. The number patterns emit bright blue light under 365 nm UV excitation. When the light source is turned off, the “4637″ pattern displayed by phosphorescence can be seen. Therefore, CA-MA-3 is suitable for high-quality anti-counterfeiting inks, thereby protecting identity privacy or hiding important information.

In summary, CA-MA composites based on CACDs embedded MA-derived crystals were synthesized quickly and effectively through the one-step hydrothermal method from MA and CA. The effective covalent crosslinking enables CACDs to be firmly embedded in the MA-derived crystals. CA-MA-3 not only enjoys an ultra-long RTP lifetime of 760 ms but also a relatively high absolute QY of 11.24%, with strong phosphorescence stability in the solution environment. Further studies have shown that the formation of amide bonds and hydrogen bonds hinders the vibration and rotation of molecules, effectively inhibits the non-radiative decay of triplet excitons, and promotes crossover between systems. Based on the good RTP properties of the CA-MA-3 composite, the as-prepared material shows a good application for solid anti-counterfeiting security. Therefore, the emergence of this material provides a larger development platform for expanding the application of RTP materials under harsh conditions.

Bingfeng Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing; Wansong Sun: Conceptualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft; Qian Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Changli Lü;: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.22271044) and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Foundation (No.20210101404JC).
